answer:
We test the premises as true and the conclusion false.  
The conclusion ~(E ≡ F) is false when E and F have the same truth value.  
If E, F, and G are all true, the first premise E ⊃ (F ∨ ~G) becomes T ⊃ (T ∨ F) = T, and the second premise F ⊃ (~E ∨ ~G) becomes T ⊃ (F ∨ F) = F, so the premises are not all true — this is not a valid counterexample.  

Checking option C: E true, F true, G false gives first premise T ⊃ (T ∨ T) = T, second premise T ⊃ (F ∨ T) = T, and conclusion ~(T ≡ T) = F, so all premises true and conclusion false.  

\boxed{C}
